name: mint cilantro chimichurri
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS. Both share the mint + cilantro + raw allium + vinegar base with a macerate-then-blend technique. The variants differ on the citrus/warm-spice slot (indian) versus additional fresh herb (occidental).

=== SHARED PREP (BOTH VARIANTS) ===

1. Finely dice white onions and garlic.
2. Toss the diced onions and garlic in JUST ENOUGH red wine vinegar to coat and lightly moisten them (see notes — do NOT submerge). Add a pinch of salt and a small pinch of sugar. Let macerate for at least 15-30 minutes at room temperature — this tames the raw allium bite and starts building the acid-and-onion base of the sauce.

=== VARIANT A — INDIAN (LIME + GINGER) ===

INGREDIENTS:
- macerated white onions + garlic (from shared prep)
- red wine vinegar (from the macerate)
- large handful of fresh cilantro
- large handful of fresh mint
- juice of 1 lime
- fresh ginger (thumb-tip knob, peeled)
- salt
- olive oil (as additional liquid — see notes)

PROCEDURE:
1. Combine everything (macerated onion-garlic-vinegar, cilantro, mint, lime juice, ginger, salt) in a food processor.
2. Pulse to a rough paste — not smooth, you want visible herb pieces.
3. Drizzle olive oil in with the motor running until the sauce reaches spoonable-but-still-thick consistency (loose paste, not a smooth vinaigrette).
4. Taste and adjust salt.

=== VARIANT B — OCCIDENTAL (PARSLEY, NO LIME OR GINGER) ===

Closer to a classic argentinian chimichurri but with mint and cilantro alongside the parsley. No lime, no ginger — this variant leans on the vinegar-and-herb axis alone.

INGREDIENTS:
- macerated white onions + garlic (from shared prep)
- red wine vinegar (from the macerate)
- large handful of fresh cilantro
- large handful of fresh mint
- large handful of fresh parsley
- salt
- olive oil (as additional liquid — see notes)

PROCEDURE:
1. Combine everything (macerated onion-garlic-vinegar, cilantro, mint, parsley, salt) in a food processor.
2. Pulse to a rough paste — visible herb pieces.
3. Drizzle olive oil in with the motor running until the sauce reaches spoonable-but-still-thick consistency.
4. Taste and adjust salt.
notes:
TWO VARIANTS ON A CHIMICHURRI-SHAPED SAUCE: both are raw green condiments built on macerated onion + vinegar + herbs + olive oil, blended in a food processor. Variant A takes it in an indian direction with lime and ginger; variant B stays occidental with parsley and no citrus/warm-spice.

MACERATE THE ONIONS AND GARLIC IN VINEGAR FIRST — LOAD-BEARING STEP: the pre-macerate is what makes this sauce work as a raw condiment. Raw onion and raw garlic straight into a food processor with fresh herbs produce something aggressive and unbalanced — the alliums dominate everything. A 15-30 minute vinegar macerate does two things: it tames the raw allium bite and pre-integrates the acid into the onion tissue so the sauce reads as unified rather than layered. Don't skip.

DON'T OVER-VINEGAR THE MACERATE — EASY FAILURE MODE: it's easy to add too much red wine vinegar at the macerate step. This is NOT a caprese dressing — you're not trying to completely annihilate the onion bitterness by drowning the onions in acid. Just enough vinegar to coat and lightly moisten the diced onions is enough; the onions should be wet but not submerged, and there shouldn't be a pool of vinegar sitting under them. Over-macerating pushes the finished sauce past the pleasant-tang threshold into aggressively-sour territory that no amount of olive oil or salt can walk back. Err on the side of less vinegar; you can always add a splash more into the blender at the end if the sauce needs more acid.

A PINCH OF SUGAR IN THE MACERATE: a small pinch of sugar goes in with the salt and vinegar. Balances the raw acid, encourages the onion to sweat properly, and rounds the finished sauce. Not enough to make it sweet — just enough to lift the vinegar's edge.

OLIVE OIL IS ADDITIONAL LIQUID — ADDS BODY AND MOUTHFEEL: olive oil goes in during the blend (not with the macerate) to give the sauce its final texture. Enough oil to make it spoonable but still thick — this is a paste that should hold its shape when scooped, not a pourable vinaigrette. The olive oil also carries the herb flavors on the palate.

RED WINE VINEGAR IS PRIMARY — WHITE WINE VINEGAR SECONDARY: red wine vinegar's tannin and depth are the right acid for this sauce. White wine vinegar works as a substitute but reads lighter and more citrus-adjacent; use it if it's what you have but expect a somewhat cleaner (and less structural) result.

=== VARIANT A NOTES (INDIAN) ===

LIME + GINGER PUSHES THE SAUCE INDIAN: lime's bright citrus and ginger's warm rhizome character land the sauce in indian condiment territory (think mint-cilantro chutney with vinegar and onion structure). This variant pairs especially well with indian proteins and vegetables — see master mint cilantro skillet for the canonical use case.

NO PARSLEY IN VARIANT A: parsley works in variant B and pushes the sauce more argentinian; keep it out of the indian variant where the cilantro + mint pair should stand alone.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (OCCIDENTAL) ===

CLOSER TO CLASSIC CHIMICHURRI — BUT NOT QUITE: authentic argentinian chimichurri is parsley-and-oregano-forward, with vinegar and olive oil. Variant B here borrows the technique but subs in the mint + cilantro trio as the herb base with parsley alongside. Reads herbaceous and green-forward without the citrus/warm-spice register of variant A.

NO LIME, NO GINGER IN VARIANT B: adding either pushes the sauce toward variant A. If you want indian character, use variant A cleanly.
